coredata.dc:title: Magnetic two-dimensional Ca-Al layered double hydroxide/Fe3O4@dextran nanocomposites as drug delivery systems
coredata.dc:creator: Molaei, Mohammad Jafar
coredata.dc:description: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are one of the two-dimensional (2D) anionic clay materials that have received great attention for drug delivery applications due to their high surface-to-volume ratio and drug loading efficiency. In this research, Al-Ca layered double hydroxides (LDHs) were synthesized in situ in presence of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles through the co-precipitation method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies showed that by increasing the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles content, the peaks are broadened due to the intercalation. Field-emission electron microscopy (FESEM) images showed the LDH nanosheets are decorated with Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles. The saturation magnetization for the sample LDH-Fe3O4@dextran was found to be 13.7 emu/g. The LDH-Fe3O4@dextran sample could load doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) with a loading efficiency of 69 %. The in vitro studies showed that the drug release from the synthesized nanocomposite is pH-sensitive. The accumulative release of DOX could reach 59 % in an environment with a pH of 7.4 and 71 % in an environment with a pH of 5.5 within 24 hrs. The synthesized magnetic nano-carrier is promising for its application as a drug delivery nanocarrier with a faster drug release in the tumor microenvironment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: A1. Biomaterials
A1. Characterization
A1. Low dimensional structures
B1. Inorganic compounds
B1. Nanomaterials
B2. Magnetic materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the synthesis method of the LDHs/Fe3O4@dextran/DOX nanohybrid.
The synthesized Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles; (a) XRD pattern and (b) TEM images.
XRD patterns of the LDHs, LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1), and LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S2).
The FTIR spectra of (a) LDH, (b) LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1), and (c) LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1)/DOX.

(a) The FESEM images of the synthesized LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1) nanocomposite showing uniform and fine distribution of the nanosheets. (b) The morphology of the S1 nanocomposite at a higher magnification. (c) The morphology of the S2 sample. (d) The FESEM image of the S1 sample and corresponding elemental map analysis.
Magnetic hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles and LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1 and S2) samples. The inset is the hysteresis loops of the samples at low applied fields.
The DOX release profiles of LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1 and S2) nanohybrids in environments with pH of 5.5 and 7.4 up to 24 hrs.
A comparison of the current research to other similar works in the application of LDHs in drug delivery systems.

head.ce:title: Magnetic two-dimensional Ca-Al layered double hydroxide/Fe3O4@dextran nanocomposites as drug delivery systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood 3619995161, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shahrood University of Technology, Shahrood 3619995161, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are one of the two-dimensional (2D) anionic clay materials that have received great attention for drug delivery applications due to their high surface-to-volume ratio and drug loading efficiency. In this research, Al-Ca layered double hydroxides (LDHs) were synthesized in situ in presence of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles through the co-precipitation method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies showed that by increasing the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles content, the peaks are broadened due to the intercalation. Field-emission electron microscopy (FESEM) images showed the LDH nanosheets are decorated with Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles. The saturation magnetization for the sample LDH-Fe3O4@dextran was found to be 13.7 emu/g. The LDH-Fe3O4@dextran sample could load doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) with a loading efficiency of 69 %. The in vitro studies showed that the drug release from the synthesized nanocomposite is pH-sensitive. The accumulative release of DOX could reach 59 % in an environment with a pH of 7.4 and 71 % in an environment with a pH of 5.5 within 24 hrs. The synthesized magnetic nano-carrier is promising for its application as a drug delivery nanocarrier with a faster drug release in the tumor microenvironment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Anticancer chemotherapeutics makes some problems in the human body when they are delivered through conventional drug delivery systems including high toxicity, poor specificity, and drug resistance. Therefore, the therapeutic value of the anticancer drug decreases due to these problems [1]. An ideal drug carrier should carry a high dose of the drug, provide intracellular release of the drug, be stable in the physiological environments, prevent drug degradation while transporting it, the surface of the carrier should remain stable, and should be biodegradable and biocompatible [2].

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are one of the two-dimensional (2D) anionic clay materials that have received great attention in recent years. LDHs are hydrated structures that are consisted of stacked face-to-face layers with positive electric charge and negatively charged species in the interlayers [3]. They have the general formula of
[ M
1 - x
2 +
M
x
3 +
( O H )
2
(
A
n -
)
x / n
]
x +
.mH2O in which part of the divalent metal cations (e.g., Cu2+, Zn2+,Ni2+, Mg2+) coordinated octahedrally by hydroxyl groups have been substituted with trivalent metal cations (e.g., Ga3+, Fe3+, Al3+) [4].

LDHs have a positive total charge since the negative charge of the anions helps the positively charged brucite-like sheets to be held through electrostatic attractions [5]. The A n
- anions between the LDH layers could be replaced with pharmaceutical compounds or other analytes [6]. The LDHs have found versatile applications including catalysis [7], adsorbents [8], inorganic nanofillers [9], supercapacitors [10], and pharmaceuticals [11]. LDHs have also found various biomedical applications including delivery carriers of genes, drugs [12], vaccines [13], antimicrobials [14], bioimaging [15], and photodynamic therapy [16]. For example, biomolecules, genes, and drugs show improved intracellular delivery efficacy when they are delivered by LDH nanoparticles [17].

The lamellar structure of the nanostructured LDHs is suitable for the drug molecules to access the layered channel instead of the interlayer anions. This gives the LDHs excellent drug loading and releasing characteristics [18]. The performance of the LDHs in biological environments depends on the particle size, surface properties, layer charge density, and chemical composition. Therefore, the drug release characteristics such as time and rate of release are influenced by the synthetic parameters [3].

The synthesis methods for LDHs are sol–gel, hydrothermal synthesis, urea hydrolysis, and co-precipitation [19]. The most conventional method for the synthesis of LDHs is low saturation coprecipitation. In this approach, a certain ratio of the metal salt solutions is mixed and by the dropwise addition of alkali solution, the LDHs are formed after aging during the stirring [20].

Magnetic nanoparticles have been used widely for magnetically targeted drug delivery due to their magnetic properties, ease of surface modification, and low bio toxicity. The drug can be loaded on the magnetic nanoparticles and the loading can be enriched on the coated shell of the nanoparticles [21]. Superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) have been investigated by researchers for their biomedical applications in drug delivery systems. In the drug delivery systems that take advantage of the SPIONs, by the application of a localized external magnetic field, site-specific delivery of the drug can be achieved. The magnetic response of the SPIONs is strongly influenced by the saturation magnetization and their physiochemical properties. The saturation magnetization of the fabricated nanocarriers should be high enough to facilitate the movements of the nanoparticles into the specific sites [22].

Different surface coatings have been applied for magnetic nanoparticles in biomedical applications including starch, chitosan, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), polyethylene glycol (PEG), alginate, albumin, and dextran. Among them, dextran is a suitable polymer for the coating of magnetic nanoparticles due to its non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and degradability. Furthermore, colloidal stability and drug delivery characteristics of the magnetic nanoparticles increase by surface coating with hydrophilic organic polymers such as dextran. Dextran is a hydrophilic water-soluble polymer consisting of mostly α-1, 6-glycosidic linkages which are physically absorbed onto magnetic nanoparticles in alkaline solutions. Hydroxylic groups can also be cross-linked to dextran and therefore be able to bind to targeted ligands [23].

DOX is an anthracycline antibiotic and is widely applied for treating a variety of solid malignant tumors. The hydrochloride form of DOX is fluorescent and water-soluble. The fluorescence properties allow monitoring of the loading and release process. DOX inhibits the formation of biomacromolecules by intercalating with DNA. DOX molecule with different functional groups, including acid and basic functional groups, and the aromatic rings are both amphiphilic and amphoteric [24].

The drug molecules can be loaded in the interlayer space of the LDHs. This can protect the drug and promote sustained release. LDHs have a high specific surface area which is favorable for drug loading. Furthermore, cellular uptake can be enhanced since the cell membrane is negatively charged and the LDHs are positively charged. Nevertheless, LDHs suffer from having adequate magnetic properties to be applied in magnetic targeting or environmental magnetic absorbents. Therefore, we aimed to use SPIONS on LDH layers to build magnetic nanocomposites of the LDHs [25]. Drug loading on LDHs has been reported by other researchers in recent years [26,27]. However, conjugating of the Ca-Al LDHs with coated SPIONS and their application as drug delivery systems has not been reported before. In this research, dextran-coated Fe3O4 SPIONs-loaded Ca-Al LDHs were synthesized through in-situ coprecipitation and their magnetic and drug delivery characteristics were investigated. The LDHs with a high surface area can load and release higher amounts of the anti-cancer drug, while the magnetic characteristics make it possible to be guided in the human body by a magnetic field. Fig. 1 is a schematic illustration of the synthesis method of the LDHs/Fe3O4@dextran/DOX.
Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O),
and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) were purchased from Merck. Dextran was provided by Sigma-Aldrich.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by the coprecipitation method. For the synthesis of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, 0.6 g FeCl2·4H2O, and 1.6 g FeCl3·6H2O were finely ground in a mortar and were added to 50 ml deionized water into a beaker and stirred until complete dissolution of the iron chlorides. Then the solution was transferred into three-neck round-bottom flasks and 30 ml of NH4OH solution was added dropwise for 45 min under the flow of argon gas to prevent the oxidation of the nanoparticles. The suspension of the precipitated particles was aged for 80 min by keeping the flask in an oil bath at 75–80 °C. The magnetic precipitates were washed several times. An external magnet was used for the collection of the precipitates in water each time. Then, the nanoparticles were dispersed in a 5 % dextran solution at 75 ˚C and stirred for 5 h. The precipitates were collected and washed using an 11000-rpm centrifuge.

Synthesis of LDHs/Fe3O4@dextran was derived from the previous report [28]. Briefly, for the synthesis of LDHs/Fe3O4@dextran, a certain weight of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles was dispersed in 25 ml of a solution of deionized water and methanol with Vdeionized water/Vmethanol = 1:1. The suspension was sonicated for 10 min. Meanwhile, a solution of 0.45 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O in 25 ml of deionized water and another solution of 0.85 g of Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O in 25 ml of deionized water were prepared. Both salt solutions were stirred at room temperature for 30 min to assure a complete solution of the salts. Then the salt solutions were added to the recently sonicated Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles suspension. Subsequently, a solution of 0.32 g Na2CO3 and 0.48 g NaOH was added dropwise to the final suspension of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles and salts solution to reach a pH of 10 under vigorous stirring. Then the suspension continued stirring for 1 h followed by 15 min sonication. The precipitates were collected with a 4000-rpm centrifuge and washed several times to reach neutral pH. The precipitates were dried at 50 °C overnight. This procedure was performed for Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles weights of 0.05 g and 0.14 g where the samples were labeled S1 and S2, respectively. For the synthesis of bare LDH nanosheets, the same procedure was performed without the addition of the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles suspension to the final solution.

The XRD patterns of the samples were recorded by a Philips PW3710 XRD machine operating with Cu-Ka radiation. The TEM images were recorded by a Philips CM30 TEM. For the preparation of the sample, a drop of the nanoparticles suspension in ethanol was dropped on the carbon-supported Cu grid after sonication of the suspension for better dispersion. The FTIR spectra of the samples were recorded on a Rayleigh WQF-520 FTIR. The magnetic hysteresis of the samples was measured by a magnetometer (Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir, Iran). The maximum saturation magnetization at 8 kOe was considered as the saturation magnetization. The size and morphology of the powder samples were studied with a Zeiss Sigma 300- HV FESEM. The samples were coated with gold (Au) using the sputtering technique before the imaging. The UV–vis absorption of the samples was performed with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 25 UV–vis spectrometer.

For the study of the drug loading efficiency of the synthesized nanohybrids, 20 ml PBS solution was collected, and 0.6 g of the nanoparticles were added and dispersed in the solution. Then, 2 ml of DOX drug was added to the prepared nanoparticles in PBS. The concentration of DOX in the drug was 2 mg/ml. The suspension container was covered with Al foil to be protected from light and stirred overnight. Then, by using a filter paper the drug-loaded nanoparticles were collected and washed to remove the unbonded drug. UV–vis spectroscopy was applied for the determination of the amount of the loaded drug. This was conducted by measuring the absorbance of the supernatant. The drug loading percent can be calculated by the below formula.
(1) Drug loading (%) = (Ma-Mb)/Ma × 100

where Ma and Mb are the initial amounts of the drug that was added to the suspension and the amount of the unbonded drug in the supernatant. The concentration calculations were performed with the help of a calibration plot of the UV–vis absorption as a function of DOX concentration [29]. To achieve the DOX concentration calibration plot, the absorption at 490 nm [30] was recorded on the UV–vis spectrometer (Fig. S1).

The drug release of the synthesized nanocomposite was measured at pH values of 7.4 and 5.5. For measuring drug release, 0.3 g of the drug-loaded nanocomposite was added to a 15 ml PBS solution and dialyzed by the application of a dialysis tube with MWCO of 12 kDa. The dialysis tube was immersed in 40 ml of PBS solution under stirring at 37 °C in dark. The same experiments were performed for two different pH values of 7.4 and 5.5. An aliquot of the PBS solution on the outside of the dialysis tube was collected at different time intervals and the same amount of the fresh PBS solution was added to the experiment container. The concentration of the DOX in the collected samples was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy and with the help of the DOX concentration calibration plot. The DOX release percent was determined by UV–vis spectroscopy according to the below formula:
(2) DOX release percentage = (V × Ct)/Z × 100 %
in which, V is the volume of the sample, Ct is the concentration of the drug in the sample of time t, and Z is the initial amount of the drug at t = 0.

The XRD pattern of the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles and their TEM images are illustrated in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern reveals the successful synthesis of the Fe3O4 phase. The TEM images exhibit that the synthesized nanoparticles have an average particle size of 18 nm. The TEM image also reveals that the nanoparticles are successfully coated with dextran.

Fig. 3 is an illustration of the XRD patterns of the LDHs, LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1), and LDHs-Fe3O4@dextran (S2). The XRD patterns of the synthesized LDHs exhibit symmetric, narrow, strong lines at low 2θ values and less symmetric and weaker peaks at higher values of 2θ in the XRD pattern. The high-intensity (00 l) reflections combined with broad peak shapes are characteristics of hydrotalcite with small crystallites and high crystallinity [31]. It can be seen that by the synthesis of the LDHs in presence of the 0.05 g Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles, the peaks have become weaker and the (003) line has shifted to a lower angle which is an indication of higher-layer spacing. By increasing the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles to 0.14 g, the magnetite peaks can be better recognized. The initial LDHs lines are broadened and some disappeared which could be the result of decreased crystallinity due to the intercalation process. The higher content of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles could be the reason for a higher degree of intercalation which could be concluded from the XRD pattern.

The FTIR spectra of LDH, LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1), and LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1)/DOX are illustrated in Fig. 4 . The FTIR spectrum of LDH shows peaks at 3636 cm−1 and 3473 cm−1 which are assigned to HOH bending vibration of the interlayer water molecules and the –OH stretch of the hydroxide layers, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy is very sensitive to the presence of carbonate anions in the LDHs. The observed weak band could enable one to conclude that the synthesized LDHs are free of the absorbed carbonates from the air. The bands that are appeared between 500 and 900 cm−1 are related to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of -Al-O-Ca and -Ca-O bonds that can illustrate the formation of an oxide network [32]. The observed peak at 423 cm−1 and 527 cm−1 are assigned to metal–oxygen-metal and metal–oxygen stretching bonds in the hydrocalumite layer of the LDHs, respectively [33]. The peaks at 786 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 also are attributed to the bonds between metals (Ca and Al) and oxygen [34]. The absorption band at 1386 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of NO3 – and that at 1620 cm−1 is related to the bending vibration of the OH bond in Ca2Al-NO3 [35]. The FTIR spectrum of the LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1) shows a band at 576 cm−1 which is assigned to the Fe-O vibration of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [36]. The peak at 3465 cm−1 is due to the water molecules absorbed on the nanocomposite. The peaks at 873 cm−1 and 773 cm−1 display the characteristics of absorption of dextran. The absorption bands at 2926 cm−1, 1416 cm−1, and 1386 cm−1 are also assigned to the dextran molecule's vibrational modes [37]. For the DOX-loaded sample, the peak around 1416 cm−1 has shifted to 1424 cm−1 which might be due to the bonding of DOX with nanocomposite. Decreasing the intensity of the peaks at 3622 cm−1 and 3675 cm−1 also could be related to the presence of DOX on the nanocomposite. The intensity of the peaks around 2935 cm−1 that are related to CH vibration is also increased [38]. Dextran possesses functional hydroxyl (–OH) groups that can be used for chemical conjugation with other materials [39]. The –OH groups of the dextran molecule can help the Fe3O4@dextran to conjugate to the LDH layers. Furthermore, the DOX hydroxyl groups can be used to enhance the loading of the drug on the LDHs/Fe3O4@dextran nanohybrid.

The morphology and elemental map of the LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1 and S2) nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that the nanocomposites have a uniform and fine distribution of the nanosheets. The image of S1 at higher magnification in Fig. 4b shows that the delaminated sheets have thicknesses in the nanometer range. The nanosheets of S1 and S2 samples are decorated with Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles. It can be seen that Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles are well distributed between the nanosheets. The elemental analysis also shows a nearly uniform distribution of the elements that contribute to the structure of the LDHs and Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles.

Fig. 6 shows the magnetic hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles, LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1), and LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S2) samples. The Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles show superparamagnetic characteristics with a high saturation magnetization of 42.5 emu/g. The nanocomposites of LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S1) and LDH-Fe3O4@dextran (S2) are also superparamagnetic with saturation magnetizations of 5.6 and 13.7 emu/g, respectively. Therefore, the synthesized magnetic nanocomposites have still preserved their superparamagnetic behavior but at a lower saturation magnetization.
The DOX drug could be loaded 65 % and 69 % on the synthesized LDH-Fe3O4@dextran S1 and S2 samples, respectively.

Physiological interactions such as (π-π) stacking, electrostatic, physisorption, and hydrophobic interactions might be responsible for loading the drug on the nanohybrid [40]. The in vitro pH-controlled release profiles of the drug from the LDH-Fe3O4@dextran S1 and S2 samples can be seen in Fig. 7 . The drug release experiments were conducted in environments with pH of 5.5 and 7.4 up to 24 hrs. The release profiles show that the cumulative release for the S1 sample in the environment with a pH of 5.5 is 68 % in 24 hrs. The drug release of the S1 sample is 56 % in the environment with a pH of 7.4 in 24 hrs. These values for the S2 sample are 71 % and 59 % in the environment with a pH of 5.5 and 7.4, respectively.

Since the environment with a pH of 5.5 resembles the acidic tumor microenvironment, it can be concluded that the drug release from the nanocomposite is faster in the tumor microenvironment. Therefore, the synthesized nanohybrid is promising as a magnetic drug carrier for drug delivery to tumor sites in the human body.

Table 1 compares the current research with other similar works in the application of LDHs in drug delivery systems. It can be seen that compared to other works, the synthesized nanohybrid not only shows sustained drug release but also is enhanced by magnetic properties which is advantageous in magnetic targeting.

In this research, Al-Ca layered double hydroxides (LDHs) were synthesized in situ in presence of Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles. The Fe3O4@dextran nanoparticles were able to be incorporated between the LDH nanosheets. The LDH-Fe3O4@dextran sample showed a saturation magnetization of 13.7 emu/g. In vitro drug loading and releasing studies showed that DOX can be loaded on LDH-Fe3O4@dextran nanocomposite with a loading efficiency of 69 %. The accumulative release of DOX could reach 59 % in an environment with a pH of 7.4 and 71 % in an environment with a pH of 5.5 within 24 hrs. Based on the findings, the synthesized magnetic LDH nanosheets are promising for application as magnetic targeting drug delivery systems. The faster drug release in an acidic environment, which mimics the tumor microenvironment, is advantageous for drug delivery systems in cancer therapy.
Availability of data and materials
The datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Mohammad Jafar Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to appreciate the Shahrood University of Technology and the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council for their financial support of this project.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcrysgro.2023.127186.
